parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I keep remembering how effortless everything used to feel, like we just naturally fell into step without even trying. Now it feels like I am walking on eggshells every single time we are in the same room. I try to bring up small things, but the air just gets heavy and the conversation dies out completely. It has been dragging on like this for months, and I am just exhausted from constantly guessing what went wrong. I miss the easy laughter we used to share before everything got so complicated.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really painful. It’s exhausting to feel like you have to monitor every word and still not know what changed. Missing the easy, natural version of the relationship can make the current tension feel even heavier.

What you’re describing also makes a lot of sense emotionally: when something used to feel safe and effortless, the silence and awkwardness now can feel like a kind of loss. And the guessing game itself can wear you down.

If it feels possible, a direct but gentle conversation may help more than trying to decode every interaction on your own. Something simple like:

- “I feel like things have been tense between us for a while, and I miss how things used to feel.”
- “I don’t want to keep guessing what’s wrong. Can we talk about it honestly?”
- “I care about this relationship, and I want to understand what changed.”

If talking in the moment feels too hard, writing it down first can help you be clear without getting pulled off course.

Also, if you want, I can help you figure out:
- how to start that conversation,
- what might be causing this distance,
- or how to cope if the other person won’t open up.

turns.user_message: The silence between us has stretched into something so thick that I barely recognize the person I used to know. I find myself replaying old memories just to remind myself that we actually used to be this close. Every attempt to fix the distance just ends up highlighting how far apart we really are now. I am tired of pretending that everything is fine when the tension is practically vibrating off the walls. It feels like I am holding onto a ghost of what we had while the reality just keeps slipping away.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really painful. You’re describing a kind of grief that can happen even when no one has officially “left” — the relationship is still there, but the closeness is gone, and that can feel incredibly lonely.

It also makes sense that replaying old memories hurts and comforts you at the same time. They remind you what was real, but they also make the current distance feel even sharper. And pretending everything is fine when it clearly isn’t must be exhausting.

If you want, a few things might help clarify what you’re carrying:
- Are you hoping to repair this connection, or are you trying to figure out whether it’s time to let go?
- Has anything specific changed, or has it just slowly faded?
- Is there one honest conversation you’ve been avoiding because you’re afraid of what it might confirm?

Sometimes the hardest part is naming the truth out loud: “I miss you, and this isn’t okay with me anymore.” That can be a starting point whether the relationship is saved or not.

If you want, I can help you think through what to say to them, or just sit with this with you a little longer.

turns.user_message: I look at the space between us and it feels like an entire lifetime has passed since we actually understood each other. The easy rhythm we once shared has completely shattered into this exhausting cycle of walking on eggshells and second-guessing every single word. I keep waiting for a spark of the old warmth to return, but it just feels like I am staring at a locked door. This heavy ache has settled so deep in my chest that I barely remember what it feels like to just breathe easy around them. I am just so incredibly tired of mourning something that is still technically right in front of me.
turns.model_response:
That sounds painfully lonely. When someone is still *there* but the closeness is gone, it can feel like you’re grieving in public—trying to hold onto a relationship that no longer feels safe or alive in the way it used to.

The “walking on eggshells” part especially stands out. That kind of tension is exhausting because you’re not just missing the good times—you’re constantly bracing yourself, managing every word, and carrying the weight of what might go wrong. Of course you’re tired. Of course it hurts.

It also makes sense that you’d feel stuck mourning something that hasn’t fully ended. That in-between space can be brutal: no clean breakup, no clear repair, just the ache of absence while the person remains close enough to keep the hope alive.

If you want, we can talk through one of these:
- what changed between you,
- whether there’s any real path back to feeling safe,
- or how to get through the day when the grief feels this heavy.

You don’t have to carry this alone here.
